prompt: A kindergarten early childhood special educator notices that one child reaches several language milestones later than same-age peers. What is the most appropriate first interpretation?
choices: Development follows broad patterns, but individual variation requires information from multiple sources and settings before conclusions are drawn.
Any difference from a class average proves a disability.
The child should immediately receive a fixed disability label.
Chronological age no longer matters when planning instruction.
rationale: Milestones provide useful reference points, not stand-alone diagnoses. Educators should document functioning across domains, contexts, and time and involve qualified team members before attributing a pattern to disability. For 5692 preparation, apply the decision to developmentally appropriate, family-centered services from birth through age eight and distinguish IDEA Part C IFSP processes from Part B IEP processes when relevant.

Lower every academic expectation for the young child.
Treat the difference as deliberate noncompliance.

Whether the disability category would provide more services.
Whether one English test falls below average.
Whether limited English proficiency or inadequate instruction is the primary explanation, using culturally and linguistically appropriate evidence.
rationale: Eligibility decisions must not mistake language acquisition, cultural difference, or lack of appropriate instruction for disability. Evidence across languages, instruction, progress, and contexts is necessary. For 5692 preparation, apply the decision to developmentally appropriate, family-centered services from birth through age eight and distinguish IDEA Part C IFSP processes from Part B IEP processes when relevant.

Lower the entire curriculum without targeted teaching.

Treat all incorrect answers as the same kind of error.
